lines.target: Words that connect two ideas are called relatives (by the way, your relatives are your aunts, cousins, etc.).
lines.base: Las palabras que vinculan dos ideas se llaman relativos (a propósito, vuestros parientes (relatives) son vuestros tíos, primos, etc.).

lines.target: Two such words that people use quite often are which and that.
lines.base: Dos de estas palabras que la gente tiene muy presentes son which y that. Ejemplos:

lines.target: The invention that won the prize was a tilt-rotor aero-engine; and
lines.base: El invento que (that) ganó el premio era un motor de avión giratorio; y

lines.target: The invention, which won the prize, was developed by the American company Howlett.
lines.base: el invento, el cual (which) ganó el premio, fue perfeccionado por la compañía americana Howlett.

lines.target: One's first reaction may be: What's the difference? But look again at these sentences: and pick out the vital information.
lines.base: La primera reacción de uno puede ser: ¿Qué diferencia hay? Pero mire otra vez estas frases: y elija la información más importante.

lines.target: In the first example, we define the invention (it is the invention that won the prize),
lines.base: En el primer ejemplo, definimos el invento (es el invento el que ganó el premio),

lines.target: whereas in the second example, the fact that the invention won the prize is additional information.
lines.base: mientras que en el segundo ejemplo, el hecho de que el invento ganara el premio es una información complementaria.

lines.target: The fuel consumption of the car is very high, which worries me.
lines.base: El consumo de combustible del coche es muy alto, lo cual me inquieta.

lines.target: The houses, which belonged to the municipal authorities, were badly in need of repair.
lines.base: Las casas, que pertenecían a la municipalidad, tenían necesidad urgente de reparación.

lines.target: The houses that belonged to the authorities were dilapidated; all the others were in a good state of repair.
lines.base: Las casas que pertenecían a las autoridades estaban estropeadas; las otras estaban en buen estado.

lines.target: Here is the project that he has been working on for the last seven months.
lines.base: Aquí está el proyecto en el que lleva trabajando siete meses.

lines.target: The project, which he has been working on for seven months, will be discussed by the research committee tomorrow.
lines.base: El proyecto, en el que él trabaja desde hace siete meses, se discutirá mañana por el Comité de investigación.

lines.target: One last point — if the relative is present in a defining clause, we can leave it out if we want.
lines.base: Una última cosa — si el relativo está presente en una oración restrictiva, podemos desecharlo si queremos.

lines.target: The house I looked at last month is worth another twenty thousand pounds.
lines.base: La casa (que) yo miré el mes pasado vale ahora veinte mil libras más.

lines.target: You cannot do this if the words are separated by two commas: The house, which I bought last year, is now worth £80,000.
lines.base: No puedes hacer esto si las palabras están separadas por dos comas: La casa, que compré el año pasado, vale ahora 80.000 libras.

gap_candidates.target: Words that connect two ideas are called relatives (by the way, your relatives are your aunts, cousins, etc.).
The invention that won the prize was a tilt-rotor aero-engine; and
The invention, which won the prize, was developed by the American company Howlett.
One's first reaction may be: What's the difference? But look again at these sentences: and pick out the vital information.
In the first example, we define the invention (it is the invention that won the prize),
whereas in the second example, the fact that the invention won the prize is additional information.
The houses, which belonged to the municipal authorities, were badly in need of repair.
The houses that belonged to the authorities were dilapidated; all the others were in a good state of repair.
One last point — if the relative is present in a defining clause, we can leave it out if we want.
